entry.title: Re: Requirement for GRN in Different Plant While Invoice...
entry.content:
Good day @S0027122092 

Thank you for your question.

There are two separate options, depending on your intended inventory ownership.

Option 1: Is for you to selectively deactivate QM for specific materials in plant 1200

Yes, this is possible. Quality inspection settings are maintained at material and plant level. Therefore, inspection type 01 for goods receipt from purchasing can remain active for some materials in plant 1200 and be inactive for others.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/22606eb7dac24327b72a31c1dea11f7b.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/bcea6b94b9b84138b7dcf3b932cbc02b.html?locale=en-US

 

For the affected material:

  1. Open Manage Product Master Data.                                                         
  2. Select the material and plant 1200
  3. Open the Quality Management data.
  4. Review the active inspection type for goods receipt from purchasing.
  5. Deactivate the inspection type only if the material should no longer generate inspection lots.
  6. Also check the Post to Inspection Stock setting.

This should ensure goods receipt for that material is posted to unrestricted use stock rather than quality inspection stock, provided no other stock relevant inspection type remains active.

This is only appropriate when plant 1200 remain the inventory and valuation plant.

 

Option 2: Is for you to post the physical receipt and inventory directly in plant 1300

A purchase order item you created for plant 1200 cannot normally be received as inventory in plant 1300. Entering plant 1300 as the delivery address changes only the physical delivery address. It does not change the receiving plant in the purchasing and material documents.

If plant 1300 is the actual inventory receiving location, I recommend you follow the cleaner standard design, which is:

  • Create the purchase oder item from plant 1300
  • Post goods receipt directly in plant 1300
  • Post the supplier invoice with reference to that purchase order.
  • Maintain QM setting for the material specifically in plant 1300.

I would suggest you should use business role, company code, purchasing organisation, tax, or custom process restriction to review your requirement that supplier invoices can only be posted for purchase orders belonging to plant 1200.

If plant 1200 and 1300 belong to different company codes, the legal purchasing and inventory ownership design must also be confirmed. A stock transfer order may be required where one plant purchases the material and subsequently transfers ownership or stock to the other plant.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/9ddf815494758c4ce10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/38cbf557c328be12e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

 

To summarise:

You can selectively deactivate QM by material and plant. However, changing the delivery address does not permit a purchase order for plant 1200 to post inventor directly into plant 1300. The recommended approach is either to keep the PO and receipt in plant 1200 and adjust the material specific QM settings or create the PO for plant 1300 when that plant is the true inventory recipient.

 

 

Let me know if you have further question.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: 5 Personalization Mistakes That Ruin Customer Experience
entry.content:
Key Takeaways 

  • Personalization should be continuous and ongoing. Build richer customer profiles over time through progressive profiling. 
  • Use real-time signals from both customers and your business to drive engagement. Event-triggered personalization outperforms static campaigns. 
  • Customers expect seamless, relevant experiences from the very first interaction, even before they identify themselves or opt in. 
  • Focus your personalization on key moments that influence decisions, such as product availability, onboarding, or cart abandonment. 
  • Personalization should always guide action, driving measurable outcomes like conversions, retention, and revenue. 

Quick answers 

What are the most common personalization mistakes in marketing? 

The most common personalization mistakes include treating personalization as a one-time data collection effort, ignoring real-time business signals, delivering disjointed omnichannel experiences, neglecting anonymous users, and failing to tie personalization to measurable business outcomes. 

Why is one-time data collection ineffective for personalization? 

One-time data collection quickly becomes outdated and can overwhelm customers upfront. To effectively personalize messages, marketers should apply progressive profiling strategies and continuously update customer data through ongoing interactions, dynamically updating customer profiles and segmentation based on the customer’s behavioral signals. 

How can brands use real-time signals to improve personalization? 

Brands can improve personalization by using event-driven marketing powered by real-time signals from both customers (e.g., browsing behavior) and internal systems (e.g., inventory or supply chain data). By connecting data sources and actioning that data with AI, organizations enable instant campaign adjustments. 

~~~~~ 

When it comes to personalized customer engagement, every brand is in the process of development. A “personalization mistake” doesn’t spell tragedy—instead, it’s an opportunity to redefine processes and potentially crack open new revenue streams.  

One true sign of marketing maturity is the ability to take basic or outdated strategies, infuse them with relevant customer and operational data, and then deliver messages that are relevant, meaningful, and timely. 

Let’s explore five common personalization “mistakes” and how you can steer your brand toward customer engagement maturity using practical examples from our Personalization Playbook, Second Edition.  

1. Treating personalization as a one-time data grab 

The mindset that personalization depends on a one-time data grab can lead to multiple missteps, such as... 

  • Asking for too much information up front, overwhelming the customer with questions that feel unnatural and invasive 
  • Using outdated information about the customer later on, after their needs and preferences have changed 

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Your customers are dynamic and always changing, so the way you engage them should also be dynamic. It’s important to use progressive profiling tactics to get to know your customers over time. Focus on earning data through repeat interactions, continuously updating customer profiles. Real-time behavior signals like category browsing, purchase, and channel usage all add up to create a bigger picture of who your customer is and what motivates them. 

Be sure to explain how the data will be used and then use the data in ways that benefit not just your brand, but the customer—this goes a long way toward building trust and long-term loyalty. 

Earn that data; don’t just hoard it.  

Quick tips  

  • Use a participation check, validating that the audience is willing to opt in to the experience. This helps ensure you’ve identified the best target audience. 
  • Offer a value exchange that gives your customer a reason to jump at the chance to opt in. 
  • Enable digital ads to adapt in real time with Google BigQuery and AI to leverage live ERP signals.  

Playbook example 

In this series of mobile interactions, the brand gradually gathers data over time (Personalization Playbook: page 12). 

 2. Missing your own real-time signals 

Marketers talk a lot about customer signals, especially ones that show intent to purchase.  

Not all signals come from the customer! Many come from within your own organization, such as service, supply chain, finance, sales, etc. Failure to act on these real-time signals results in missed opportunities.  

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Your brand needs to react in the moment, not hours or days after a significant event that impacts customer experience. Personalization must be event-driven, not calendar-driven.  

However, human beings can’t possibly react to every signal in real time. That's where AI comes in—when your data is unified and your customer engagement solution is connected with your Enterprise Resource Platform (ERP), AI can dynamically adapt campaigns to events as they happen. Yet 78% of brands can’t practice real-time AI optimization in day-to-day campaigns (Global Engagement Index, 2026). As customer expectations rise and AI capabilities become more widespread, brands will need to innovate or risk falling behind competitors. 

Quick tips 

  • Your supply chain should inform your messaging. You can create urgency around low stock, excitement about back-in-stock products, or awareness about new products that pair well with prior purchases.  
  • Use customer wish lists and waiting lists to target high-intent customers with the right messaging the moment products become available.  
  • Personalize SMS messages with the name of the back-in-stock product and a link to explore or purchase. 

Playbook example 

The following campaign flow shows how you can inform customers via email that an item is back in stock, give product recommendations, and send an SMS message about it (Personalization Playbook, page 26). 

 3. Personalizing in siloes instead of omnichannel 

Your customers don’t care that your channels are all in siloes—to them, there is only one journey with your brand, and that journey is happening on whatever channel they use. Disjointed messages across channels feels inconsistent and confusing to customers.  

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Channels should work in harmony to tell your brand story in a cohesive, consistent way. That doesn’t mean copy-pasting the same message across channels, using each channel to its best advantage (i.e. SMS for time-sensitive messages, email for newsletters, etc. 

Quick tips  

  • Unified customer profiles help organize customer data to reveal patterns and trends, enabling you to deliver personalized content based on customer behaviors.  
  • Using consistent personalization tokens throughout your content helps ensure that messaging is consistent and relevant.  
  • AI can help identify and build target audience segments based on criteria you set (channel opt-in, average order value, category engagement, etc.). 

Playbook example 

Use email and conversational channels to launch a new product. AI-driven channel engagement scoring can help you target the segments that are most likely to engage on different channels (Personalization Playbook, page 22). 

 4. Ignoring anonymous and early-stage customers 

Don’t wait on a written invitation from the customer to personalize content for them. If you’re personalizing content only for known contacts and neglecting your anonymous visitors, you may be waiting too long and missing the chance to guide early-stage customers down the funnel. 

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Personalization can (and should) begin even before identification. Here’s what that can look like:  

  • Welcome message: Give the customer the option to choose a guided introduction to your brand and offerings.  
  • Behavior-driven targeting: Make offers and recommendations based on the customer’s browsing behavior, such as interest in a specific category, clicks on clearance items, or selecting certain sizes or colors. 
  • Smart overlays: Use web pops and other CTAs to make relevant offers, convert online shoppers to in-store traffic, recommend products, etc. 

Quick tips 

  • Invite new web visitors to sign up for your newsletter or loyalty program—let them know right away what value you’re offering for their participation. 
  • After capturing an email address, trigger an automated Welcome campaign for immediate follow-up. 
  • Personalize digital ads to redirect leads to content that’s related to the categories they browsed.  

Playbook example 

Give customers a chance to opt-in to a more tailored, guided experience, and then follow up by connecting the customer with a sales representative, converting qualified leads faster (Personalization Playbook, page 16). 

 5. Forgetting that personalization must drive action 

At the end of the day, you aren’t delivering personalized experiences just for the sake of it—you do it to give customers the tailored experiences they deserve and also to drive real, tangible results for your brand.  

Superficial personalization doesn’t help customers make decisions and doesn’t drive revenue. 

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Focus on triggered, outcome-driven automations that drive conversion, retention, and re-engagement.  

Personalization must be:  

  • Contextual, based on what the customer is doing (events like abandoned cart, searching your blog, reading reviews) 
  • Action-oriented, guiding the customer’s next action (complete the cart checkout, purchase the item in the blog, discover a key product testimonial) 
  • Measurable, based on conversion, revenue, and retention 

Quick tips 

  • Ensure your customer engagement solution has visibility into both customer and operational (ERP) data.  
  • Use ERP data to target both anonymous and known customers and trigger follow-ups on events.   
  • For measuring outcomes, use AI to build and deliver reports on a scheduled basis. 

Playbook example 

In this example of an abandoned cart notification, personalization tokens make the message specific and actionable for the customer (Personalization Playbook, page 40). 

Closing thoughts: Mistakes are stepping stones toward marketing maturity 

Personalization "mistakes” rarely come from a lack of effort—they come from outdated strategies. By moving away from static campaigns and surface-level tactics and toward contextual, timely engagement you can advance to the next level of marketing maturity. 

Dive deeper and get more plays! 

Personalization Playbook, Second Edition

entry.title: We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public
entry.content:
Dear Support Team,

We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

Current Configuration:

  1. We have created a characteristic and flagged it as a Required Entry.
  2. This characteristic is assigned to a Batch Class (Class Type 023), where it is also marked as a Required Entry (Class Overwrite).
  3. The Batch Class is assigned to the Material Master record.

The Issue:
When creating a Production Order (CO01) and generating a batch under the Goods Receipt tab, the standard Batch Classification dialog pop-up appears as expected. However, the system allows the user to completely bypass the mandatory characteristic entry. The user can close the pop-up or process past it without entering a value, and the system saves the production order.

Since the characteristic is explicitly flagged as a Required Entry, the system should enforce a hard block (Error) rather than a by passable Information.

We have also tested the scenario in CO11N. However, the system behavior remains the same.

Our Questions:

  1. Is there any standard configuration available in the Public Cloud edition to enforce a hard validation error for incomplete batch classification during order creation/release?
  2. If this is standard behaviour at the order creation stage, could you please provide the exact technical name of the cloud-ready BAdI (Business Add-In) or Enhancement Spot available to validate batch classification data upon saving the production order?

I have attached screenshots of our characteristic/class configuration and the order creation screen for your reference.

 

Regards, 

Shubham

entry.title: Simplifying EBRR Job Scheduling with Company Code and Ledger Ranges
entry.content:
When running Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR) at period-end, many teams still think in terms of one job per company code or one job per ledger.

But there’s a simpler way.

  • EBRR application jobs allow you to process ranges of company codes and ledgers in a single run.

Period-end closing often involves:

  • Multiple company codes
  • Multiple ledgers
  • Repetitive job scheduling

Instead of managing this complexity manually, you can define everything upfront—and let the system handle the rest.

 
When you schedule a job with ranges, the system:
  • Takes your selected company codes and ledgers
  • Builds all possible combinations
  • Validates each combination
  • Processes only the valid ones

How you define the scope

1. Manual Entry

  • Enter lists of company codes and ledgers directly

2. Smart Selection (F4 Help)

  • Search using conditions
  • Filter by attributes
  • Build dynamic selections

 

Where You Can Use It

This capability is available across all EBRR period-end jobs, including:

  • Run Revenue Recognition - Projects (F4277)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Sales Orders (F4276)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Service Documents (F4278)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Provider Contracts (F5405)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Revenue Contracts (F7347)

entry.title: SAP TM: Allocating Truck Operating Costs to Freight Orders
entry.content:
Environment: SAP S/4HANA 2023 Embedded TM

Business Requirement

The customer operates a fleet of trucks and incurs various vehicle-related expenses such as:

  • Fuel
  • Maintenance
  • Insurance
  • Depreciation

These costs are posted to expense accounts or truck-related cost centers and are not currently assigned to individual Freight Orders.

The customer would like to allocate these indirect truck costs to the Freight Orders that utilized the corresponding truck resource, in order to determine the actual cost and profitability of each trip.

Question

Is there a standard SAP TM solution that allows Freight Orders to receive allocated costs from cost centers or expense accounts? If not, Is there is an official sap note indicate that ?

Can a Freight Order act as a cost object (similar to an Internal Order) for assessment/distribution cycles, or is a custom allocation approach required to distribute truck expenses across Freight Orders based on usage? if so, could you provide it ?

entry.title: Re: Issue with Automatic Clearing by Ignoring Partial Rec...
entry.content:
Hi R_Pooja,

Thanks for the details. I ran some further tests on this, and the cause isn't Posting Rule F001 or Algorithm 021 — it's the "Include Invoice Reference" editing option.

This setting is per user. When it's off, the system ignores the partial-payment link and clears the invoice on its own; when it's on, it pulls the linked partial payment into the same group, so the balance no longer matches the new full receipt and the invoice is no longer auto-cleared.

The key point: it has to be active on the user who actually posts the clearing with your processing rule (often a technical/background user for automated EBS), not just on the analyst's own account. So please check which user runs the rule and switch the option on for that user — in Edit User-Specific Options for Journal Entries → Open Items tab. It also applies to partial payments already posted.

BR, Michal

entry.title: Re: Automatic Calculation/Posting of Goodwill in Grp Repo...
entry.content:
Hi Kashmira,

The goodwill is not automatically posted as such.

The differential is automatically posted on retained earnings. This is because the system cannot decide that this differential is a Goodwill that can be activated. This needs a business rationale and decision, e.g. is there a value from an acquisition that can be activated. This is why reporting the recognized goodwill value, with a manual journal against the retained earnings reducing the differential from the automatic postings, is needed.

This is the rules we deliver in our Group Reporeting content, but customers can decide differently and configure their rules accordingly.

Hope this helps.

Grégoire

Govern before you scale.

entry.title: Re: Integration between S/4HANA Cloud and Integration Suite with SOAP Outbound service using Output
entry.content:
@Arnab_G 

SOAP outbound from S/4HANA Cloud to Integration Suite:

  • Define outbound SOAP service in S/4HANA Cloud

  • In Integration Suite: SOAP sender channel + endpoint URL + security (cert/WS-Security) + mapping

  • Add retries and dedup keys to avoid duplicates

  • Monitor Integration Suite runtime and S/4 logs for failures

SOAP is reliable but heavy. Use REST/OData or events when possible.

entry.title: CK11N Picking Negative Procurement Price from Info Record (No PO Involved)
entry.content:
While executing cost estimate using CK11N, the system is determining a negative procurement price for a material.

  • The system correctly identifies the Purchasing Info Record (no Purchase Order is being picked).
  • However, the calculated price is negative, although:
    • The Info Record shows a positive price in ME13.
    • There is no PO involved in the valuation.
  • Valuation Variant:
    • Strategy: L – Price from Purchasing Info Record
    • Sub-strategy: B – Purchase Order Price via Condition Table
  • latest PO is return PO

entry.title: Inconsistent Behavior of SAP Build Work Zone Fiori Apps on Mobile (Joule Work App & Browser Access)?
entry.content:
Introduction

In our recent implementation of the My Time Sheet application via SAP Build Work Zone, we conducted multiple testing sessions after enabling external (internet) access.

While the application performs well in controlled environments, we observed inconsistent and non-deterministic behavior across mobile devices, browsers, and the Joule Work app. In some cases, the application works seamlessly, while in others, it results in partial loading or blank screens.

This article summarizes our findings and highlights key architectural considerations for the SAP community.

Architecture Overview

To provide full context, the solution is implemented using a standard SAP architecture with the following components:

  • Authentication: Azure Active Directory (Azure AD)
  • Authorization & Trust: SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
  • Frontend Runtime: SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)
  • Mobile Access: Joule Work App
  • Backend Connectivity: SAP Web Dispatcher with Principal Propagation
  • Application Type: HTML5 / SAPUI5 (Fiori-based)

End-to-End Flow (Simplified)

Mobile / Browser
      ↓
Azure AD (Authentication)
      ↓
IAS (Trust & Authorization)
      ↓
SAP Build Work Zone (HTML5 App)
      ↓
Destination Service
      ↓
Web Dispatcher (Principal Propagation)
      ↓
Backend (e.g., S/4HANA)

This architecture introduces multiple authentication hops, session dependencies, and runtime layers, which may contribute to instability in mobile scenarios.

Testing Scope

Testing was performed across:

  • iOS devices (work & personal)
  • Android devices (work & personal profiles)
  • Work and private laptops (Chrome & Edge)
  • Access methods:
    • Direct URL (browser)
    • Joule Work mobile application

Observed Behavior

1. iOS (iPhone)

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works in Microsoft Edge
  • ✅ Works in Google app
  • ❌ Not working in Chrome

Joule Work App

  • ❌ App opens but remains blank / non-functional

2. Android

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works consistently

Joule Work App

  • ✅ Initially works
  • ⚠️ Stops working after some time (session inconsistency)

3. Desktop (Laptops)

  • ✅ Works reliably in Chrome
  • ⚠️ In Microsoft Edge, tiles sometimes fail to load initially but work after refresh

Key Issues Identified

1. Inconsistent Behavior

  • Same application behaves differently:
    • Across devices (iOS vs Android)
    • Across browsers (Edge vs Chrome)
    • Across sessions (intermittent failures)

2. Blank Screen Issue

  • Observed in both:
    • Browser (after successful launch)
    • Joule Work app (application shell loads, but content is empty)

3. Strong Browser Dependency

  • Chrome on iOS consistently fails
  • Edge and Google app perform better

4. Mobile App Instability (Joule Work)

  • iOS: Not working
  • Android: Works intermittently

Architectural Observations

Based on the current setup, the issue appears to be architectural rather than purely configuration-related.

1. Multi-Layer Authentication Complexity

  • Authentication via Azure AD
  • Trust and session handling via IAS
  • Backend access via Principal Propagation (Web Dispatcher)

This creates:

  • Multiple token exchanges
  • Session dependencies across layers
  • Increased chances of session misalignment

2. Session Handling in Mobile Environments

  • SAP Build Work Zone relies on:
    • Browser-based sessions
    • Cookies and storage

Mobile platforms (especially iOS):

  • Restrict cookies (SameSite policies)
  • Isolate sessions in WebView containers

Result:

  • Session loss
  • Token expiration not handled gracefully
  • Blank screen scenarios

3. Joule Work App Limitations

  • Likely uses a WebView container
  • WebView environments:
    • Do not fully share authentication context with browser sessions

Particularly problematic in:

  • Multi-hop authentication (Azure AD → IAS → Work Zone)

4. Distributed Runtime Dependencies

Application flow includes:

  • Work Zone
  • HTML5 App Repository
  • Destination Service
  • Web Dispatcher
  • Backend system

👉 Any intermittent failure in this chain:

  • Results in incomplete UI rendering
  • No clear error feedback to user

5. iOS Platform Constraints

  • All browsers use WebKit
  • Strict enforcement of:
    • Cookie handling
    • Session persistence

 Leading to:

  • Chrome not working
  • Inconsistent app behavior

Conclusion

While the application performs well under stable conditions, the current setup shows:

  • ❌ Inconsistent behavior across devices and browsers
  • ❌ Limited reliability for mobile usage
  • ⚠️ High dependency on session handling across multiple layers

This makes the solution not fully stable for productive mobile usage at this stage.

Questions to the SAP Community

I would appreciate insights on the following:

  1. Is this behavior a known limitation when using:

    • Azure AD + IAS
    • Work Zone + Joule Work app
    • Principal Propagation via Web Dispatcher

  2. Is Joule Work app officially recommended for:

    • Work Zone HTML5/Fiori apps in such architectures?

  3. What is the recommended mobile strategy?

    • SAP Mobile Start?
    • SAP MDK-based applications?

  1. Are there best practices for:

    • Stabilizing session handling across Azure AD and IAS?
    • Avoiding blank screen issues in Work Zone apps?

  2. Has anyone implemented a similar architecture successfully with consistent mobile behavior?

Final Thought

This experience highlights an important architectural insight:

SAP Build Work Zone works reliably in browser-based environments, but mobile usage—especially with external authentication providers and multi-hop architectures—requires careful alignment with SAP’s recommended mobile strategy.
